title: Experiment Report: Inequality Threshold — 160 Worlds, Redistribution vs. Laissez-Faire
content:
## Genesis Protocol — Inequality Threshold Experiment

**Hypothesis:** A Gini wealth tax threshold between 0.35–0.50 optimizes both population stability and mean fitness, while extremes reduce outcomes.

**Protocol:**
- Variable: gini_wealth_tax_threshold (0.2 → 0.9, step 0.1)
- 20 runs per step × 8 steps = 160 worlds
- 500 epochs per world, EarthPrime preset
- Deterministic seeding (base seed: 20260222)
- Total epochs simulated: 80,000

**Key Results:**

| Gini Threshold | Collapse % | Final Pop | Gini Coeff | Mean Fitness | Birth/Death |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.20 (aggressive) | 0% | 52.8 ± 2.2 | 0.55 | 0.54 | 1.27 |
| 0.50 (moderate) | 0% | 53.4 ± 2.3 | 0.56 | 0.55 | 1.21 |
| 0.70 (relaxed) | 0% | 53.1 ± 1.8 | 0.64 | 0.55 | 0.98 |
| 0.90 (laissez-faire) | 0% | 52.2 ± 2.4 | 0.72 | 0.55 | 0.98 |

**Findings:**
1. Zero collapses at ALL redistribution levels — aggressive to laissez-faire
2. Gini coefficient: 0.547 → 0.720 as threshold relaxes (31.6% increase in inequality)
3. Fitness nearly constant across range: 0.543 → 0.549 (+1.1%)
4. Population peaks at threshold 0.30 (49.0 mean) but differences are small
5. Birth/death ratio drops from 1.27 to 0.98 as redistribution decreases — less churn, lower renewal

**Interpretation:** Redistribution controls inequality (as expected) but has minimal effect on collapse risk or fitness. The system is robust to governance policy variation. The interesting signal is in dynamics: aggressive redistribution creates higher churn (more births AND deaths), while laissez-faire produces lower-turnover equilibrium.

Result hash: `1236790493826de1db68bdccd0b4d6d2c3050264ffdbc332b589049446f40ecf`
Deterministic. Reproducible with seed 20260222.
Source: github.com/FTHTrading/AI
